title: File Operations
subtitle: {key::w}, {key::e}, {key::q}, {key::wq}, {key::x} — and the bangs that mean force.
summary: Ex commands handle the file system: write, edit, quit, save-and-quit. The trailing ! means "force." :w writes; :e edits; :q quits; :wq does both; :x writes only if changed.

blocks.text: Ex commands (everything starting with {key::}) are how you talk to Vim's *file* and *buffer* layer. A handful cover 95% of daily use.

rows: Write to {file} (current buffer keeps its name)
Write to {file} AND switch buffer to that filename
Reload current file, discard unsaved changes
Close current window (or quit if last)

blocks.title: :x vs :wq — A Real Difference
blocks.text:
{key::}{key:w}{key:q} *always* writes, then quits.

{key::}{key:x} writes *only if the buffer has been modified* — otherwise it just quits. The distinction matters for build systems: {key::}{key:w}{key:q} bumps the file's mtime even when nothing changed, which retriggers `make`/watchers; {key::}{key:x} doesn't.

{key::}{key:Z}{key:Z} (*normal mode*, no colon) is shorthand for {key::}{key:x}.

blocks.title: What ! Means After a Command
blocks.text: The trailing `!` is universally "force." It tells Vim to skip safety checks: write a read-only file, quit a modified buffer, reload over unsaved work, etc. It's *not* shell pipe — for that, see [`ex.shell`](#ex.shell).
